title: Aging in Autism: Brain, Motor, and Memory Markers of Neurodegenerative Risks

summary.hook: This poster investigates biological and behavioral signs of increased neurodegenerative vulnerability in aging autistic adults using multimodal evidence: MRI cortical morphometry...
summary.summary_30s: This poster investigates biological and behavioral signs of increased neurodegenerative vulnerability in aging autistic adults using multimodal evidence: MRI cortical morphometry linked to executive/motor/language performance, Parkinson’s disease (PD) polygenic risk and clinician-rated parkinsonism, and Alzheimer’s disease (AD)-related blood biomarkers plus longitudinal verbal memory.

key_numbers: Not stated on poster overall; some analyses report degrees of freedom (e.g., t(134), t(25)).
Biennial follow-up for participants ≥40; exact years not stated.
Autistic and non-autistic adults without intellectual disability, spanning early adulthood through older age; autism...
Orbitofrontal surface area correlated with executive/motor/fluency measures in ASD (e.g., Trails A r=-0.33 p=0.0096).
PD-PGS higher in autistic adults (t(134)=2.98, p=0.003).
takeaways: Background claim: Middle-aged and older autistic adults are 2.6× more likely to be diagnosed with early-onset Alzheimer’s disease and 6× more likely to be diagnosed with...
Conclusion: Across MRI, genomic, and proteomic modalities, findings point to frontal–motor system vulnerability and elevated neurodegenerative risk in aging autism.

general: What is the main takeaway you hope attendees remember from this poster?
What surprised you most once you started digging into this topic?
Why does this work matter beyond this specific poster session?
undergrad: What part of this project would be most helpful for an undergraduate student to learn from?
What skills or tools were most important for getting this project done?
If an undergraduate wanted to get involved in work like this, where would you suggest they start?
grad: What were the exact sample sizes per aim, and how much overlap was there across MRI, genomics, proteomics, and longitudinal cognition?
What was the key design or analysis tradeoff in this project?
If you were extending this into a next study, what would you tighten or measure differently?
researcher: What were the exact sample sizes per aim, and how much overlap was there across MRI, genomics, proteomics, and longitudinal cognition?
What was your recruitment strategy for autistic and non-autistic adults, and how did you ensure comparability between groups (age/sex/education)?
For Aim 1, what specific cortical parcellations/regions correspond to the “left frontal” and “motor” findings (beyond orbitofrontal/precentral)?
clinician: How could these findings affect clinical care, screening, or decision-making in practice?
What kind of patient or care setting would this poster be most relevant for?
What would need to happen before this work would be ready for broader clinical use?
community: Who in the community is most likely to benefit if this work moves forward?
What kinds of community partnerships would strengthen the next phase of this project?
What would meaningful real-world impact look like for the people touched by this work?
leader: What type of support, infrastructure, or investment would help this work move further?

If a leader wanted to back the next step, what outcome would be most important to track?
